title: Botanical Microfossil Extraction from Paleontological Sediments
author: Megan C. O'Toole

steps.title: Place the bag into a mortar...
steps.instruction: Place the bag into a mortar and use a pestle to crush the samples (as finely as possible). This works best if some air is kept in the inside bag to allow the sample to move during grinding. Image 2: sections separated into double bags with sample codes.

steps.title: Pour the ground powder through a...
steps.instruction: Pour the ground powder through a funnel into sealable falcon test tubes for storage.

steps.title: Transfer sample to a centrifuge tube,...
steps.instruction: Transfer sample to a centrifuge tube, filling to 1/3 of the height to allow volume for chemicals.

steps.title: In this protocol approximately 0
steps.instruction: In this protocol approximately 0.5 mL of sediment were processed per sample in 2.0 mL centrifuge tubes. If more sediment is desired, larger centrifuge tubes will be necessary. This is applicable for sediments with a lower concentration of microfossils.

steps.title: If the sample contains carbonates, they...
steps.instruction: If the sample contains carbonates, they will have to be removed for quality viewing of the microfossils. To remove carbonates, start by pouring 50 mL of 30% concentration of Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) into a small beaker. Complete these steps in a covered fumehood, wearing proper personal protective equipment: gloves, lab coat, safety goggles, etc.

steps.title: Carefully pipet a small amount of...
steps.instruction: Carefully pipet a small amount of 30% Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) into each centrifuge tube, and stir with a clean pipet to further expose carbonates to acid. If carbonates are present, there can be a vigorous exothermic reaction. Use an ice bath if necessary to control the reaction. Stir gently to avoid a violent reaction. Keep an ice bath in fumehood to cool samples immediately in case of a violent reaction (by placing samples in ice water).

steps.title: Pipet more 30% Hydrochloric Acid (HCl)...
steps.instruction: Pipet more 30% Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) to fill the tube, leaving enough room to close the top. Again, add the acid slowly to avoid bubbling over.

steps.title: Stir again with a clean pipet tip
steps.instruction: Stir again with a clean pipet tip.

steps.title: Wait until the bubbling stops and the reaction ceases
steps.instruction: Wait until the bubbling stops and the reaction ceases.

steps.title: If a reaction is present but...
steps.instruction: If a reaction is present but slow (low rate of bubbling), heat the samples on a hotplate to a maximum temperature of 70 °C until the bubbling ceases.

steps.title: Allow the samples to cool and stir
steps.instruction: Allow the samples to cool and stir.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, °C, for...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, °C, for 00:10:00 to pellet the sediment.

steps.title: Carefully pipet the supernatant into a...
steps.instruction: Carefully pipet the supernatant into a 1000 mL waste beaker half-filled with water.

steps.title: Fill the centrifuge tube with distilled...
steps.instruction: Fill the centrifuge tube with distilled water (dH 2 O) , and repeat steps 7 and 8 to rinse the sample. Be sure to remix the sample with a pipet tip to unpellet before centrifuging again.

steps.title: Repeat until the supernatant is clear, approximately two times
steps.instruction: Repeat until the supernatant is clear, approximately two times.

steps.title: Dispose of waste Hydrochloric Acid by...
steps.instruction: Dispose of waste Hydrochloric Acid by flushing down the sink while cold water pours from the tap. Leave the water running for 00:05:00 .

steps.title: Dry the samples by repeating step 9 with Acetone
steps.instruction: Dry the samples by repeating step 9 with Acetone . Pipet the supernatant into a 100 mL waste beaker.

steps.title: After decanting the waste Acetone ,...
steps.instruction: After decanting the waste Acetone , place samples and the 100 mL beaker of decanted acetone in the fumehood to evaporate for 1-2 days. After 3 days, wash out now empty beaker.

steps.title: If the sample contains organics, they...
steps.instruction: If the sample contains organics, they will have to be removed for quality viewing of the microfossils. To remove organic material, start by filling the centrifuge tubes halfway with 30% concentration Hydrogen Peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) . Complete these steps in a covered fumehood, wearing proper personal protective equipment: gloves, lab coat, safety goggles, etc. As organics are destroyed, the solution's color will change from black to red/brown to orange to a clear yellow/green and emit hazardous fumes.

steps.title: Put centrifuge tubes on a hot...
steps.instruction: Put centrifuge tubes on a hot plate and warm to 40 °C . If the reaction is vigorous, remove from heat and place in an ice bucket until reaction has ceased.

steps.title: After approximately 00:30:00 to 01:00:00 ,...
steps.instruction: After approximately 00:30:00 to 01:00:00 , increase the temperature to 80 °C . If reaction is none or minimal then increase temperature to 100 °C . Stir carefully with a pipet tip every 5 minutes. Once no reaction is visible, move to step 15 .

steps.title: Completely fill the centrifuge tubes with...
steps.instruction: Completely fill the centrifuge tubes with 30% Hydrogen Peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) . As organics are destroyed, the solution's color will change from black to red/brown to orange to a clear yellow/green.

steps.title: If the reaction has not ceased...
steps.instruction: If the reaction has not ceased within 8 hours, leave on hot plate overnight at 80 °C . Top off with 30% Hydrogen Peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) in the morning if evaporated.

steps.title: When the samples are a clear...
steps.instruction: When the samples are a clear yellow or yellow/green, remove from heat and allow to cool.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, , 10 minutes to pellet
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, , 10 minutes to pellet.

steps.title: Remove supernatant with pipet, rinse with...
steps.instruction: Remove supernatant with pipet, rinse with water, and dry with Acetone by repeating step 11.

steps.title: After carbonate and organic steps are...
steps.instruction: After carbonate and organic steps are completed or skipped depending on sample content, microfossils have to be isolated. This can be done using a specific gravity solution such as ZnBr 2 and centrifugation. To prepare the Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) solution, start with a magnetic stirrer and slowly add 500 g of solid Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) to 175 mL of distilled water (dH 2 O) . This reaction is exothermic and will produce heat.

steps.title: Based on literature review and trial...
steps.instruction: Based on literature review and trial and error, we generated a Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) solution with density of 2.3 g/mL using: If needing to prepare more or less solution, use the following equation to determine the volume of water and mass of Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) necessary for a density of 2.3 g/mL. The amount of solution is dependent on sample size, quantity, and sediment type.

steps.title: Below is a practical example
steps.instruction: Below is a practical example: Creating a 2.3 g/mL solution of Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) using 250 g of Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) with a 4.22 g/mL density from the manufacturer: Multiplying both sides by denominator gives: Rearranging to have like terms on each side: Dividing both sides by 1.30, leaving: ∴ To make a 2.3 g/mL solution of Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) , one should mix 250 g of solid Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) with 87.36 mL of H 2 O . Make sure to check the correct density was achieved by using a hydrometer with the correct specific gravity range.

steps.title: Stir the Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2...
steps.instruction: Stir the Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) solution and cool down for 01:00:00 to 02:00:00 hours, until room temperature or 25 °C , per a thermometer.

steps.title: Check density using a hydrometer
steps.instruction: Check density using a hydrometer.

steps.title: The specific gravity density should be 2
steps.instruction: The specific gravity density should be 2.30 ± 0.057 g/mL. If higher, add water slowly to the beaker until density decreases to the accepted range. If lower, add Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) to bring density up to the accepted range.

steps.title: Pour the solution into a glass...
steps.instruction: Pour the solution into a glass bottle, cap, and label it with the date and density.

steps.title: To separate paleobotanical microfossils from inorganic...
steps.instruction: To separate paleobotanical microfossils from inorganic sediment, first prepare a new set of labeled centrifuge tubes and a set of pipets with bulbs.

steps.title: Add dried sample to the new centrifuge tubes
steps.instruction: Add dried sample to the new centrifuge tubes.

steps.title: Fill the centrifuge tubes with the...
steps.instruction: Fill the centrifuge tubes with the Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) solution using a new pipet, leaving room so the tube can be closed.

steps.title: Gently mix with a clean pipet
steps.instruction: Gently mix with a clean pipet.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, , 5...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, , 5 minutes Heavy materials will sink, whereas microfossils will float forming a ring at the water-air interface.

steps.title: For each sample, use a new...
steps.instruction: For each sample, use a new pipet to draw off the microfossil surface layer. Pipet into the new labeled test tube. If no material is visible, draw off 0.5-1 mL of liquid anyway from as close to the surface as possible. Focus on the ring close to the tube wall. Image 3: floating microfossils in a ring at top of the solution.

steps.title: Fill a clear wash bottle with...
steps.instruction: Fill a clear wash bottle with hot non-distilled water (any source including sink will suffice). Add water to dilute the microfossil solution. Stir well with a pipet to mix water and microfossil-ZnBr 2 solution. Centrifuge at 1700 rpm, °C, 10 minutes and decant supernatant with a new pipet into a 'Waste ZnBr 2 ' container. Do not pour down the sink.

steps.title: If no evidence of floated material...
steps.instruction: If no evidence of floated material is present on the surface of the solution, it could be an indication that only a small amount of paleobotanical microfossils are present. In such case, top off the original sample tubes with Zinc Bromide (ZnBr 2 ) and do a second float separation repeating steps 24-29 .

steps.title: Repeat step 29 two more times
steps.instruction: Repeat step 29 two more times.

steps.title: Acetone wash the paleobotanical microfossils using...
steps.instruction: Acetone wash the paleobotanical microfossils using the same procedure from step 11.

steps.title: Label a glass side and carefully...
steps.instruction: Label a glass side and carefully add a drop of Immersion Oil onto a glass slide.

steps.title: Image 4
steps.instruction: Image 4: example video of slide preparation ( steps 33-36 )

steps.title: Using a clean spatula, add about...
steps.instruction: Using a clean spatula, add about a quarter of a pinky fingernail of powder from step 32 to the immersion oil on the slide.

steps.title: Mix the oil and sample with...
steps.instruction: Mix the oil and sample with the tip of a new glass pipet.

steps.title: Slowly place the coverslip on top of the oil/microfossil mixture
steps.instruction: Slowly place the coverslip on top of the oil/microfossil mixture. This is best done by touching the edge of the coverslip to the slide, then by letting it press on itself. Finally, gently push the coverslip to create a flat layer of microfossils.

steps.title: If the intention is to keep...
steps.instruction: If the intention is to keep the slide for future use rather than observing right away, seal the coverslip onto the slide with clear nail polish. This is best done by slowly placing a bead of nail polish along all the edges, letting the nail polish solution seep between the two glass layers by capillary action.

steps.title: Air dry for at least 24 hours before handling
steps.instruction: Air dry for at least 24 hours before handling.

steps.title: Repeat step 37 and 37
steps.instruction: Repeat step 37 and 37.1 for better sealing of material, if desired.

steps.title: Use a compound microscope, binocular or...
steps.instruction: Use a compound microscope, binocular or trinocular, for brightfield viewing (light placed below sample, viewing from above).

steps.title: A polarizing lens will help discern...
steps.instruction: A polarizing lens will help discern shapes and features in phytoliths and diatoms.

steps.title: A magnification of 40x is good for the initial observation
steps.instruction: A magnification of 40x is good for the initial observation. For individual identification of microfossils and microscope photography, a magnification of 100x will be required.

steps.title: Image 5
steps.instruction: Image 5: Microscope field of view of sample, magnified at 40x. Notice the very dense concentration of palaeobotanical microfossils. Scale bar (in black) is approximately 20 microns.

steps.title: Image 6
steps.instruction: Image 6: Examples of microscope photographs showing: phytoliths (a, b; 100x magnification), diatoms (c, d, e; 100x magnification), sponge spicules (f; 100x magnification), and clusters of palaeobotanical microfossils (g, h, i) at 40x magnification. All scales (in black) are approximately 20 microns.

steps.title: Using a chisel, outline a section...
steps.instruction: Using a chisel, outline a section to be used for the study. This should be done in a dust collector or a fumehood with a protective surface (e.g. aluminum foil) laid down to protect the researchers and sample.

steps.title: Prepare sediment block by cleaning the exposed sections
steps.instruction: Prepare sediment block by cleaning the exposed sections. Stratigraphical layers should be visible for best sampling strategy as per next steps. Here, our samples have layers/laminations. These layers can be separated to achieve finer stratigraphical resolution.

steps.title: Determine how thick the sampling region...
steps.instruction: Determine how thick the sampling region should be in relation to the thickness of the geological layers. If the source specimen is densely layered, then a thinner sampling region will result in a higher accuracy of placing microfossils within correct historical timelines. E.g.: varve sediments (such as the examples included here) will include years of deposition in just a few centimeters. In contrast, metamorphic samples might include single-event deposition throughout meters of stratigraphy.

steps.title: Use a flat tool to separate...
steps.instruction: Use a flat tool to separate the sample from the rock. A flat chisel can separate portions until the whole sub-sample is isolated. Image 1: example sample of first chiseled sub-sample (composed of several layers) removed. Removed layer is approximately 0.5cm in depth.

steps.title: Mass the sample
steps.instruction: Mass the sample. Make sure to maintain equal mass across sub-samples throughout separation. Depending on the material sampled the mass will vary (e.g. diatomite achieved good microfossil density at 0.5g). For optimal results maintain an accuracy of ±0.05g.

steps.title: Repeat steps 1
steps.instruction: Repeat steps 1.2-1.4 for each sample while cleaning the tools and using new aluminum foil to prevent cross-contamination.

steps.title: Transfer each sample into a new...
steps.instruction: Transfer each sample into a new plastic sealable bag, and then put that bag into a 2nd new plastic sealable bag.
